g.data-narration: Welcome to Introduction to Searching! Let's explore fundamental algorithms for finding elements in data collections.
Searching is finding a specific element or checking its existence in a collection. It's fundamental to computer science and everyday applications.
g.text: The process of finding a specific element or determining whether
a particular element exists within a collection of data.

g.data-narration: Searching enables quick data retrieval, powers database operations, and supports decision-making in programs.
g.text: • Data Retrieval: Quick access to information from large datasets
• Database Operations: Core operation in DBMS
• Decision Making: Basis for conditional logic
• Real-world: Contact lookup, web search, inventory

g.data-narration: There are two main types: Sequential Linear Search checks each element, while Interval Binary Search divides the sorted array to find elements faster.
g.text: Checks each element one by one
Divides search space to eliminate portions

g.data-narration: Linear search starts from the first element, compares each with the key, returns position if found, or 'not found' if exhausted.
g.text: 1. Start first element → 2. Compare each → 3. Return index or -1

g.data-narration: Here's the linear search implementation in C. It loops through each element, returns index on match, or -1 if not found.
Linear search has O(n) time complexity: best case O(1) if found first, worst case O(n) if at end or absent.
g.text: O(n) - At last or not found

g.data-narration: Binary search requires sorted data! It finds the middle, compares with key, then searches left or right half recursively.
g.text: Binary Search: Prerequisites & How It Works

g.data-narration: Let's trace binary search for key=15. Middle is 12 (less), so search right half. Next middle is 18 (greater), search left, found!
Binary search uses low and high pointers to track the search range, calculating mid each iteration and narrowing the range.
Binary search achieves O(log n) time complexity by halving the search space each iteration. Space is O(1) iteratively.
Key concepts include: Search Key is the value being found, Successful returns position, Unsuccessful returns -1, and comparison count measures efficiency.
Linear search works on any data O(n), binary needs sorted O(log n). Use linear for small/unsorted, binary for large sorted data.
Searching is used everywhere: databases, spell checkers, phone directories, search engines, and operating systems.
For exams: know differences clearly, write both algorithms, understand complexities, remember sorted requirement for binary search.
g.text: 1. Know difference clearly | 2. Write both algorithms | 3. Understand time complexities | 4. Binary requires sorted data | 5. Practice tracing examples
